likelihood_factors: Third-party app ecosystems increase token exposure paths
Long-lived refresh tokens amplify blast radius when stolen
Over-broad SMART scopes are common in early integrations
exploit_preconditions: Compromised partner or client application
Weak redirect URI or token handling controls
Insufficient token binding and session monitoring
detection_indicators: Unusual token-use geography or device fingerprint shifts
Rapid high-volume Patient/Observation export sequences
Scope usage anomalies versus baseline app behavior
historical_incidents: Healthcare portals and API ecosystems have repeatedly shown OAuth token misuse patterns in breach investigations

likelihood_factors: Bulk export endpoints are high-value attacker objectives
Role/scope drift over time can silently increase data access
Partner integrations often bypass strict export throttling
exploit_preconditions: Access token with export-capable scope
Insufficient patient-compartment or row-level controls
Weak anomaly detection on long-running export jobs
detection_indicators: Large FHIR export jobs outside approved windows
Repeated $export calls from unfamiliar integrations
Spike in data egress volume tied to one client identity
historical_incidents: Large healthcare breaches commonly involve over-privileged API/service accounts and bulk extraction behavior

likelihood_factors: HL7v2 lacks native message-layer authentication
Legacy interface engines frequently rely on network trust
Clinical interfaces often have broad internal reach
exploit_preconditions: Access to interface network segment or engine
Ability to craft syntactically valid HL7 segments
Insufficient schema and sender validation
detection_indicators: Unexpected ADT/ORM/ORU message bursts
Segment-level anomalies in PID/OBR/OBX fields
Atypical message sender IDs or routing paths
historical_incidents: Healthcare incident analyses frequently cite insecure integration interfaces as initial manipulation vectors

likelihood_factors: Privileged account abuse remains a persistent healthcare risk
High clinical urgency can mask subtle order tampering
Insufficient dual-control for high-risk order changes
exploit_preconditions: Compromised clinician/admin credentials
Write access to medication/allergy/order fields
Limited immutable logging or delayed review workflows
detection_indicators: Out-of-hours high-risk order modifications
Frequent allergy/medication overrides by one account
Order-change patterns inconsistent with clinician specialty
historical_incidents: Healthcare safety advisories repeatedly warn about integrity-focused attacks, not only confidentiality breaches

likelihood_factors: DICOM header de-identification is often inconsistently enforced
Research sharing workflows increase export frequency
Legacy imaging systems may lack modern data-loss controls
exploit_preconditions: Access to imaging export workflow or PACS integration
Missing metadata-sanitization policy enforcement
Inadequate outbound data review controls
detection_indicators: DICOM exports containing direct identifiers in headers
Unusual volume of external DICOM transfers
Access to imaging archives from unexpected user groups
historical_incidents: Publicly exposed PACS systems and imaging metadata leaks have been repeatedly documented in healthcare security research

likelihood_factors: Ransomware targeting healthcare remains persistent and high-impact
Flat or weakly segmented IT/IoMT networks accelerate spread
Legacy medical devices often lag patch cycles
exploit_preconditions: Initial endpoint compromise in enterprise IT
Lateral movement path into biomedical/device zones
Insufficient backup isolation and recovery rehearsals
detection_indicators: Simultaneous device-management and AD account anomalies
Rapid encryption-like I/O across clinical systems
Sudden device communication drop-offs in ICU/OR segments
historical_incidents: NHS disruption during WannaCry (2017)
Large US healthcare ransomware incidents causing procedure delays and diversion events

likelihood_factors: Medical device firmware and update channels are attractive long-dwell vectors
Supplier ecosystem complexity increases trust-chain risk
Verification controls vary widely across device fleets
exploit_preconditions: Compromised firmware signing/update infrastructure
Weak secure-boot or update signature verification
Limited SBOM-driven vulnerability governance
detection_indicators: Firmware hash drift from approved baselines
Unexpected update package provenance
Safety-alarm behavior changes after updates
historical_incidents: Multiple FDA and CISA advisories have highlighted medical-device software and firmware integrity vulnerabilities

likelihood_factors: Federated trust is brittle when implementations diverge
Cross-organization token validation can be inconsistent
Legacy identity bridges can weaken issuer assurance
exploit_preconditions: Issuer/audience validation weakness
Replay window or clock-skew tolerance gaps
Insufficient assertion-signature verification
detection_indicators: Token audience/issuer mismatches in federation logs
Unexpected assertion chains between organizations
Anomalous cross-enterprise access spikes by one identity
historical_incidents: Healthcare federation incidents frequently involve trust-configuration errors enabling unauthorized cross-domain access

likelihood_factors: Controlled-substance workflows are high-value fraud targets
Credential-sharing and weak MFA increase abuse risk
Clinical throughput pressure can reduce review quality
exploit_preconditions: Access to prescribing credentials or workflow approvals
Weak anomaly controls for controlled-substance ordering
Insufficient segregation of prescribing and dispensing duties
detection_indicators: High-risk prescribing velocity anomalies by provider
Prescription pattern shifts outside specialty norms
Mismatch between encounter records and prescription activity
historical_incidents: Healthcare and pharmacy fraud cases repeatedly involve credential abuse and e-prescribing manipulation

likelihood_factors: ML pipelines often inherit weak data provenance controls
Clinical model updates can be operationally under-governed
Safety impact is high when models influence diagnosis/therapy
exploit_preconditions: Write path into training/labeling datasets
Insufficient validation gates before promotion
Weak separation between experimentation and production assets
detection_indicators: Unexpected model-performance drift by cohort
Distribution shift in key clinical feature inputs
Disagreement spikes between model output and clinician override patterns
historical_incidents: Clinical AI safety literature and regulatory guidance highlight data governance and model integrity as central risk vectors

likelihood_factors: Consumer-grade account hygiene increases credential-stuffing success
Telehealth portals are internet-exposed and high-volume
Session-management misconfigurations are common in rapid deployments
exploit_preconditions: Compromised credentials or weak MFA coverage
Inadequate risk-based authentication controls
Weak session anomaly detection
detection_indicators: Impossible-travel and device-fingerprint anomalies
Repeated failed login bursts followed by successful access
Unusual access to message/consultation archives
historical_incidents: Telehealth and patient-portal breach reports frequently involve credential abuse and account takeover patterns

likelihood_factors: Genomic datasets have high long-term value
Research collaboration expands access surface and transfer paths
Pseudonymized data remains re-identification sensitive
exploit_preconditions: Access to research data repositories or export tooling
Weak export-approval workflows and entitlement controls
Insufficient monitoring of bulk research data pulls
detection_indicators: Large off-hours exports from research environments
Repeated access to high-value cohorts without protocol context
Unexpected cross-border data transfer attempts
historical_incidents: Research-sector breaches have repeatedly targeted high-value biomedical and genomic datasets

likelihood_factors: DICOMweb APIs increase externally reachable imaging query surface
Imaging services frequently expose broad metadata fields by default
Legacy access policies are often ported into modern API gateways without granular segmentation
exploit_preconditions: Compromised API credential or weakly protected integration account
Insufficient study-level authorization controls
Limited alerting on abnormal query/retrieve velocity
detection_indicators: High-volume QIDO/WADO calls from a single integration identity
Sequential study enumeration patterns outside normal workflows
Unexpected metadata extraction from non-radiology user contexts
historical_incidents: Repeated healthcare imaging exposures have shown that discoverable PACS/DICOM services can drive high-volume confidentiality breaches

likelihood_factors: Service-oriented device control surfaces expand command-path attack opportunities
Biomedical trust-anchor management is often inconsistent across vendors
Clinical urgency may reduce tolerance for aggressive command-path lockouts
exploit_preconditions: Ability to impersonate trusted device/orchestrator identity
Weak command authorization policy at service endpoint
Insufficient runtime provenance checks for high-risk command calls
detection_indicators: Control commands issued from atypical node identities
Unexpected command bursts near alarm state transitions
Mismatch between asserted device context and authorized role profile
historical_incidents: Medical-device advisories consistently highlight inadequate authentication and authorization in networked command interfaces

likelihood_factors: E-prescribing pathways involve multiple intermediaries and trust handoffs
Replay protections are inconsistently validated across pharmacy ecosystems
Controlled-substance demand creates persistent fraud incentives
exploit_preconditions: Access to prescription/refill message channel or compromised intermediary account
Weak nonce/timestamp validation for refill transactions
Insufficient anomaly monitoring on prescriber refill cadence
detection_indicators: Repeated identical refill requests within abnormal intervals
Refill volume spikes disconnected from encounter history
Dispense destinations inconsistent with patient baseline geography
historical_incidents: Pharmacy and healthcare fraud investigations repeatedly show replay/credential abuse in prescription workflows

likelihood_factors: Clearinghouse concentration creates systemic single-point compromise risk
Batch EDI processing can delay detection of integrity manipulation
Complex payer-provider mappings increase opportunity for subtle data tampering
exploit_preconditions: Compromise of EDI transformation or transmission path
Weak end-to-end integrity validation for X12 payloads
Limited reconciliation between source clinical events and claim output
detection_indicators: Unexpected deltas in claim adjudication outcomes by payer
X12 segment-level anomalies in recurring transaction families
Mismatch between authorized service lines and billed procedure sets
historical_incidents: Major healthcare clearinghouse disruptions have shown high operational impact from transaction-path compromise

likelihood_factors: Home-network and consumer-device conditions reduce trust assurance
Enrollment workflows can under-verify persistent device identity
High-volume remote telemetry streams complicate manual validation
exploit_preconditions: Ability to spoof or clone enrolled device identity
Insufficient attestation and key-binding in ingestion stack
Weak cross-checks between telemetry and clinical context
detection_indicators: Physiological values inconsistent with patient longitudinal baseline
Multiple device identities presenting overlapping telemetry signatures
Source-network and geolocation drift for a stable patient device
historical_incidents: Remote monitoring security assessments consistently report identity and telemetry integrity weaknesses in home-care ecosystems

likelihood_factors: Federated document exchange relies on shared trust assumptions across multiple operators
Registry metadata is high-value because it controls discoverability and retrieval routing
Cross-community governance maturity often varies between participants
exploit_preconditions: Compromised registry or integration-service credentials
Insufficient integrity controls on metadata updates
Weak cross-entity reconciliation and alerting
detection_indicators: Unexpected document metadata mutations outside approved maintenance windows
Cross-community retrieval patterns misaligned with patient encounter context
Spike in failed or rerouted document retrieval events
historical_incidents: Healthcare exchange incidents repeatedly show that identity and metadata trust-layer weaknesses can cause broad downstream exposure

likelihood_factors: Patient matching is probabilistic and sensitive to data quality drift
Cross-jurisdiction demographic normalization differences increase mismatch risk
High-throughput discovery workflows reduce manual verification opportunities
exploit_preconditions: Ability to influence patient discovery attributes or identity mapping channels
Insufficient confidence-threshold governance for automatic matches
Limited downstream verification of identity-correlation provenance
detection_indicators: Frequent identity-correlation overrides in specific organizations
Low-confidence match acceptance rates above baseline
Record retrieval requests tied to demographic combinations with high collision risk
historical_incidents: Interoperability programs and patient-safety investigations consistently identify wrong-patient matching as a high-impact risk class

likelihood_factors: Model-driven EHR stacks centralize semantic control in archetype/template governance paths
Change-management shortcuts can bypass formal safety and integrity review
Downstream systems may trust template versions without independent validation
exploit_preconditions: Administrative or governance-role compromise
Insufficient approval controls for archetype/template publication
Weak semantic regression testing before production rollout
detection_indicators: Unexpected archetype/template version changes with broad deployment impact
Clinical decision-support drift after model updates
Spike in record-validation errors tied to new template versions
historical_incidents: Health IT incident reports repeatedly note that configuration and data-model changes can create patient-safety hazards at scale

likelihood_factors: Dynamic client onboarding expands attack surface in federated health ecosystems
Trust-anchor and certificate lifecycle governance is operationally complex
Partner ecosystems may differ in registration assurance quality
exploit_preconditions: Ability to exploit registration policy or trust-anchor misconfiguration
Insufficient client vetting and attestation checks
Limited behavioral monitoring for newly registered clients
detection_indicators: New client registrations with atypical scope and audience combinations
Rapid post-registration data access bursts
Certificate chain anomalies during onboarding and token exchange
historical_incidents: OAuth ecosystem breaches across sectors repeatedly show client-registration and trust misconfiguration as common compromise enablers

likelihood_factors: Document-based exchange channels may accept high-complexity payloads from external partners
Validation often focuses on structure more than clinical semantic consistency
Legacy ingestion pipelines can propagate malformed content widely before detection
exploit_preconditions: Access to trusted inbound document channel or compromised sender identity
Insufficient signature, schema, and semantic validation controls
Lack of quarantine review for anomalous document payloads
detection_indicators: Unexpected coded-value distributions in inbound C-CDA sections
Parser warnings or transformation failures correlated with specific sources
Clinical discrepancy flags after document ingestion events
historical_incidents: Clinical-document exchange programs have repeatedly documented data quality and integrity risk from malformed external payloads

likelihood_factors: Terminology services are high-impact single points for semantic correctness across many systems
Governance workflows for value-set updates are often operationally constrained
Clinical coding and billing dependencies amplify downstream blast radius
exploit_preconditions: Compromised terminology-administration account, API token, or CI pipeline
Insufficient signing, approval, and provenance controls for value-set publication
Limited semantic regression testing before production synchronization
detection_indicators: Unexpected value-set or concept-map deltas outside approved release cadence
Sudden coding-distribution shifts in diagnoses, labs, or problem lists
CDS alert behavior drift correlated with terminology update events
historical_incidents: Patient-safety and interoperability investigations repeatedly identify terminology and mapping quality failures as high-impact root causes

likelihood_factors: Healthcare relies on concentrated third-party platforms
Trusted updates/integrations can bypass perimeter assumptions
Vendor outages can immediately impact clinical operations
exploit_preconditions: Compromise of vendor build/distribution systems
Implicit trust in update channels or integration credentials
Insufficient segmentation of vendor-connected pathways
detection_indicators: Simultaneous anomalies after vendor software updates
Unexpected privileged API calls from vendor identities
Correlated incidents across organizations sharing one vendor
historical_incidents: Healthcare-wide downstream disruptions have followed high-impact third-party compromise events, including major clearinghouse incidents
